def parse_ini_line(line):
    """Parse one INI line. Return (key, value) or None for blank/comment.
    Supports double-quoted values which may contain ';' and '='.
    A ';' outside of quotes starts a comment.
    """
    line = line.split(';', 1)[0].rstrip()
    if not line.strip() or line.lstrip().startswith('['):
        return None
    if '=' not in line:
        return None
    k, v = line.split('=', 1)
    k = k.strip()
    v = v.strip()
    if len(v) >= 2 and v[0] == '"' and v[-1] == '"':
        v = v[1:-1]
    return k, v

The current implementation strips all semicolons, including those inside quoted values. I'll fix it to only strip semicolons outside quotes by splitting on semicolons only when the value is not in quotes.

turns.response_text:
The current implementation fails to merge continuation lines. It treats each line as a separate header, even if it starts with whitespace (obs-fold), and strips the line's leading whitespace before appending the value, which breaks the continuation.

Here's a fix that merges continuation lines and preserves leading whitespace on the first line:

```python
def parse_headers(raw):
    headers = []
    lines = raw.split('
')
    current_header = None
    current_value = ''

    for line in lines:
        if not line:
            continue

        if current_header is None:
            name, _, value = line.partition(':')
            current_header = name
            current_value = value.strip()
        else:
            current_value += ' ' + line.strip()

    if current_header is not None:
        headers.append((current_header, current_value))

    return headers
```

tool_call_args.test_code:
def parse_headers(raw):
    headers = []
    lines = raw.split('\r\n')
    current_header = None
    current_value = ''

    for line in lines:
        if not line:
            continue

        if current_header is None:
            name, _, value = line.partition(':')
            current_header = name
            current_value = value.strip()
        else:
            current_value += ' ' + line.strip()

    if current_header is not None:
        headers.append((current_header, current_value))

    return headers

# Test 1: Simple single header

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def shell_tokenize(s):
    tokens = []
    buf = []
    i = 0
    in_single = False
    in_double = False
    in_token = False
    n = len(s)
    while i < n:
        ch = s[i]
        if ch == '\' and i + 1 < n:
            if not in_single and not in_double:
                buf.append(s[i + 1])
            else:
                buf.append(ch)
            i += 2
            in_token = True
            continue
        if ch == "'" and not in_double:
            in_single = not in_single
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch == '"' and not in_single:
            in_double = not in_double
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch.isspace() and not in_single and not in_double:
            if in_token:
                tokens.append(''.join(buf))
                buf = []
                in_token = False
            i += 1
            continue
        buf.append(ch)
        in_token = True
        i += 1
    if in_single or in_double:
        raise ValueError('Unterminated quote')
    if in_token:
        tokens.append(''.join(buf))
    return tokens", "test_code": "def shell_tokenize(s):
    tokens = []
    buf = []

    in_single = False
    in_double = False
    in_token = False
    n = len(s)
    while i < n:
        ch = s[i]
        if ch == '\' and i + 1 < n:
            if in_double:
                # Inside double quotes, only escape $, `, ", \, and newline
                escape_chars = {'$', '`', '"', '\', '
'}
                if s[i + 1] in escape_chars:
                    buf.append(s[i + 1])
                else:
                    # If not a special escape, append the backslash and the character
                    buf.append('\')
                    buf.append(s[i + 1])
            elif in_single:
                # Inside single quotes, append the backslash literally
                buf.append('\')
                buf.append(s[i + 1])
            else:
                # Outside quotes, escape the next character
                buf.append(s[i + 1])
            i += 2
            in_token = True
            continue
        if ch == "'" and not in_double:
            in_single = not in_single
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch == '"' and not in_single:
            in_double = not in_double
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch.isspace() and not in_single and not in_double:
            if in_token:
                tokens.append(''.join(buf))
                buf = []
                in_token = False
            i += 1
            continue
        buf.append(ch)
        in_token = True
        i += 1
    if in_single or in_double:
        raise ValueError('Unterminated quote')
    if in_token:
        tokens.append(''.join(buf))
    return tokens

# Test 2: Double-quoted string preserves spaces
assert shell_tokenize('echo "hello world"') == ['echo', 'hello world']

# Test 3: Single quotes are fully literal

# Test 9: Adjacent single+unquoted concat

# Test 11: Empty quoted string still creates an empty token

assert res == ['cmd', '', 'arg'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 12: Unterminated quote raises
try:
    shell_tokenize("echo 'never closed")
    assert False, 'Expected ValueError'
except ValueError:
    pass

print('All shell_tokenize tests passed!')

                buf.append(ch)

        if ch == "'" and not in_double:

            in_token = True

        if ch == '"' and not in_single:
            in_double = not in_double
            in_token = True
            i += 1

        if ch.isspace() and not in_single and not in_double:

# Test 1: Simple whitespace split
assert shell_tokenize('echo hello world') == ['echo', 'hello', 'world']

# Test 2: Double-quoted string preserves spaces
assert shell_tokenize('echo "hello world"') == ['echo', 'hello world']

# Test 3: Single quotes are fully literal
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'a\\nb'")
assert res == ['echo', 'a\nb'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: Backslash-space outside quotes escapes space
res = shell_tokenize('echo a\ b')
assert res == ['echo', 'a b'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: Double-quoted backslash+n is literal (n is not special)
res = shell_tokenize('echo "a\\nb"')
assert res == ['echo', 'a\nb'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 6: Double-quoted backslash+double-quote is escape
res = shell_tokenize('echo "a\\"b"')
assert res == ['echo', 'a"b'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 7: Double-quoted backslash+dollar is escape
res = shell_tokenize('echo \"$var"')
assert res == ['echo', '$var'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Mixed single+double quoted concatenation forms one token
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'hello'" world"")
assert res == ['echo', 'hello world'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 9: Adjacent single+unquoted concat
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'pre'fix")
assert res == ['echo', 'prefix'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 10: Single quotes containing a double quote
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'a"b'")
assert res == ['echo', 'a"b'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 11: Empty quoted string still creates an empty token
res = shell_tokenize("cmd '' arg")
assert res == ['cmd', '', 'arg'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 12: Unterminated quote raises
try:
    shell_tokenize("echo 'never closed")
    assert False, 'Expected ValueError'
except ValueError:
    pass

print('All shell_tokenize tests passed!')

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def parse_qs(qs):
    """Parse an application/x-www-form-urlencoded query string.
    Return a dict: str -> list[str] if the key appears more than once,
    or str -> str for single-occurrence keys. Apply + -> ' ' and %XX decoding
    to both keys and values.
    """
    from urllib.parse import unquote
    result = {}
    if qs.startswith('?'):
        qs = qs[1:]  # Remove leading '?'
    if not qs:
        return result
    parts = qs.split('&')
    for part in parts:
        if '=' in part:
            k, v = part.split('=', 1)
        else:
            k, v = part, ''  # Bare key with no '=' becomes value ''
        k = unquote(k)
        v = unquote(v)
        if k in result:
            if isinstance(result[k], list):
                result[k].append(v)
            else:
                result[k] = [result[k], v]
        else:
            result[k] = v
    return result

# Test 1: Single key-value
assert parse_qs('a=1') == {'a': '1'}

# Test 2: Two distinct keys
assert parse_qs('a=1&b=2') == {'a': '1', 'b': '2'}

# Test 3: Repeated key collects list
res = parse_qs('a=1&a=2&a=3')
assert res == {'a': ['1', '2', '3']}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: + decodes to space in value
res = parse_qs('q=hello+world')
assert res == {'q': 'hello world'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: %2B decodes to literal +
res = parse_qs('op=%2B')
assert res == {'op': '+'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 6: Percent-encoded key
res = parse_qs('a%20b=1')
assert res == {'a b': '1'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 7: Bare key with no '='
res = parse_qs('flag&a=1')
assert res == {'flag': '', 'a': '1'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Empty value
res = parse_qs('k=')
assert res == {'k': ''}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 9: Leading '?' allowed
res = parse_qs('?a=1&b=2')
assert res == {'a': '1', 'b': '2'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 10: Empty input
assert parse_qs('') == {}
assert parse_qs('?') == {}

# Test 11: Mixed repeated and unique

assert res == {'a': ['1', '2'], 'b': 'x'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 12: + inside key too
res = parse_qs('first+name=jane')
assert res == {'first name': 'jane'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 13: Both + and %XX in same value
res = parse_qs('s=a%20b+c')
assert res == {'s': 'a b c'}, f'Got {res!r}'

    """Parse an application/x-www-form-urlencoded query string.
    Return a dict: str -> list[str] if the key appears more than once,
    or str -> str for single-occurrence keys. Apply + -> ' ' and %XX decoding
    to both keys and values.
    """
    from urllib.parse import unquote
    result = {}
    if qs.startswith('?'):
        qs = qs[1:]  # Remove leading '?'
    if not qs:

    parts = qs.split('&')
    for part in parts:
        if '=' in part:
            k, v = part.split('=', 1)
        else:

        k = unquote(k)
        v = unquote(v)
        if k in result:
            if isinstance(result[k], list):
                result[k].append(v)
            else:
                result[k] = [result[k], v]
        else:
            result[k] = v
    return result

    result = {}

    return result

def parse_qs(qs):
    from urllib.parse import unquote
    result = {}
    if qs.startswith('?'):

    if not qs:

    for part in qs.split('&'):
        if '=' in part:
            k, v = part.split('=', 1)
        else:
            k, v = part, ''
        result[k] = unquote(k)
        result[k] = unquote(v)
    return result

The code is incorrect. I'll fix the bug and verify it passes.

First, the current implementation is overwriting the value of keys when they repeat because it's assigning the decoded value to the same key each time. I'll fix that by collecting values in a list for repeated keys.

Second, the code is decoding the key and value separately, but it should decode the key and value together as a single string. I'll fix that by decoding the full part (key and value) before splitting.

Here is the fix:

```python
def parse_qs(qs):
    from urllib.parse import unquote
    result = {}
    if qs.startswith('?'):
        qs = qs[1:]
    if not qs:
        return result
    for part in qs.split('&'):
        if '=' in part:
            k, v = part.split('=', 1)
        else:
            k, v = part, ''
        decoded_part = unquote(part)
        k, v = decoded_part.split('=', 1) if '=' in decoded_part else (decoded_part, '')
        result[k] = v
    return result

# Test 1: Single key-value
```

def natural_key(s):
    import re
    parts = re.split(r'(d+)', s)
    key = []
    for p in parts:
        if p.isdigit():
            key.append(int(p))
        else:
            key.append(p)
    return key

# Test 1: Basic numeric suffix ordering
assert sorted(['file1', 'file10', 'file2'], key=natural_key) == ['file1', 'file2', 'file10']

# Test 6: Pure text
assert sorted(['banana', 'apple', 'cherry'], key=natural_key) == ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# Test 7: Pure numbers (as strings)
res = sorted(['100', '20', '3'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['3', '20', '100'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Leading zero on a pure number

# Test 9: Letters and digits interleaved

# Test 10: Number at start

# Test 11: Number only vs number with suffix — numbers first
res = sorted(['2', '2a'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['2', '2a'], f'Got {res!r}'

print('All natural_key tests passed!')

tool_call_args.code:
def _parse_cidr(cidr):
    ip_str, _, plen_str = cidr.partition('/')
    plen = int(plen_str)
    octets = [int(o) for o in ip_str.split('.')]
    ip = (octets[0] << 24) | (octets[1] << 16) | (octets[2] << 8) | octets[3]
    mask = ((1 << (32 - plen)) - 1) << plen & 0xFFFFFFFF
    start = ip & mask
    end = start + (1 << (32 - plen))
    return start, end


def cidr_overlaps(a, b):
    a_start, a_end = _parse_cidr(a)
    b_start, b_end = _parse_cidr(b)
    return a_start <= b_end and b_start <= a_end

def _parse_cidr(cidr):
    ip_str, _, plen_str = cidr.partition('/')
    plen = int(plen_str)
    octets = [int(o) for o in ip_str.split('.')]
    ip = (octets[0] << 24) | (octets[1] << 16) | (octets[2] << 8) | octets[3]
    mask = (1 << (32 - plen)) - 1
    start = ip & mask
    end = start + (1 << (32 - plen))
    return start, end


def cidr_overlaps(a, b):
    a_start, a_end = _parse_cidr(a)
    b_start, b_end = _parse_cidr(b)
    return a_start <= b_end and b_start <= a_end

def _parse_cidr(cidr):
    ip_str, _, plen_str = cidr.partition('/');
    plen = int(plen_str);
    octets = [int(o) for o in ip_str.split('.')];
    ip = (octets[0] << 24) | (octets[1] << 16) | (octets[2] << 8) | octets[3];
    mask = ((1 << 32) - 1) << (32 - plen) & 0xFFFFFFFF;
    start = ip & mask;
    end = start + (1 << (32 - plen));
    return start, end;

def cidr_overlaps(a, b):
    """Return True iff CIDR ranges a and b share at least one address."""
    a_start, a_end = _parse_cidr(a);
    b_start, b_end = _parse_cidr(b);
    return a_start <= b_end and b_start <= a_end;

# Test 1: Identical networks overlap
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.0/24') is True

# Test 2: One strictly contained in the other
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.128/25') is True
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.128/25', '10.0.0.0/24') is True

# Test 3: Adjacent non-overlapping
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.1.0/24') is False

# Test 4: Disjoint
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '192.168.0.0/24') is False

# Test 5: /32 single host inside a /24
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/32', '10.0.0.0/24') is True
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.5/32') is True

# Test 6: /32 vs /32 same address
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/32', '10.0.0.5/32') is True

# Test 7: /32 vs /32 different address
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/32', '10.0.0.6/32') is False

# Test 8: /0 covers everything
assert cidr_overlaps('0.0.0.0/0', '8.8.8.8/32') is True
assert cidr_overlaps('8.8.8.8/32', '0.0.0.0/0') is True

# Test 9: Boundary — last address of first == first address of second
# 10.0.0.0/25 covers .0-.127; 10.0.0.128/25 covers .128-.255 — disjoint
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/25', '10.0.0.128/25') is False

# Test 10: Boundary — last address of first == first address of second, but overlapping by 1
# 10.0.0.0/24 covers .0-.255; 10.0.0.255/32 is the last address — overlap
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.255/32') is True

# Test 11: Non-canonical host bits tolerated

# Test 12: Far apart

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "import hashlib
class CountingBloom:
    """Counting Bloom filter with insert/remove/contains/estimate."""

    def __init__(self, size, seeds):
        self.size = size
        self.seeds = list(seeds)
        self.counters = [0] * size

    def _hashes(self, item):
        data = str(item).encode('utf-8')
        out = []
        for s in self.seeds:
            h = hashlib.sha256(s.encode('utf-8') + data).digest()
            out.append(int.from_bytes(h[:8], 'big') % self.size)
        return out

    def insert(self, item):
        for idx in self._hashes(item):
            self.counters[idx] += 1

    def contains(self, item):
        return all(self.counters[idx] >= 1 for idx in self._hashes(item))

    def remove(self, item):
        for idx in self._hashes(item):
            if self.counters[idx] > 0:
                self.counters[idx] -= 1

    def estimate(self):
        """Return the estimated number of distinct insertions currently in the
        filter, based on counter sum divided by hash count. Should round to
        nearest integer and never be negative."""
        total = sum(self.counters)
        return total // len(self.seeds)", "test_code": "seeds = ['s0', 's1', 's2', 's3']

# Test 1: Fresh filter says contains is False and estimate is 0
bf = CountingBloom(128, seeds)
assert bf.contains('alpha') is False
assert bf.estimate() == 0

# Test 2: Insert then contains
bf.insert('alpha')
assert bf.contains('alpha') is True
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 3: Insert another distinct item
bf.insert('beta')
assert bf.contains('beta') is True
assert bf.estimate() == 2

# Test 4: Remove present item makes it absent (assuming no spurious collisions)
bf.remove('alpha')
assert bf.contains('alpha') is False
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 5: Remove absent item is a no-op — counters must not change
snapshot = list(bf.counters)
bf.remove('never_inserted')
assert bf.counters == snapshot, 'remove() of absent item must not modify counters'
assert bf.contains('beta') is True, 'beta must still be present after a no-op remove'
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 6: No counter can ever be negative
for c in bf.counters:
    assert c >= 0, f'Found negative counter: {c}'

# Test 7: Insert duplicates and remove them individually
bf2 = CountingBloom(256, seeds)
for _ in range(5):
    bf2.insert('gamma')
assert bf2.contains('gamma') is True
assert bf2.estimate() == 5
for _ in range(5):
    bf2.remove('gamma')
assert bf2.contains('gamma') is False
assert bf2.estimate() == 0
assert all(c >= 0 for c in bf2.counters)

# Test 8: Removing one more time does nothing
bf2.remove('gamma')
assert bf2.estimate() == 0
assert all(c >= 0 for c in bf2.counters)

# Test 9: Independent items are preserved after absent-remove
bf3 = CountingBloom(1024, seeds)
for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']:
    bf3.insert(x)
before = [bf3.contains(x) for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']]
assert all(before)
bf3.remove('z')  # z was never inserted
after = [bf3.contains(x) for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']]
assert after == before, 'absent-remove must not affect other items'

print('All CountingBloom tests passed!')"}}

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "class SegTree:
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.n = n
        self.tree = [0] * (4 * max(n, 1))
        self.lazy = [0] * (4 * max(n, 1))

    def _apply(self, node, l, r, v):
        self.tree[node] += (r - l) * v
        self.lazy[node] += v

    def _push(self, node, l, r):
        if self.lazy[node] != 0:
            m = (l + r) // 2
            self._apply(2 * node, l, m, self.lazy[node])
            self._apply(2 * node + 1, m, r, self.lazy[node])
            self.lazy[node] = 0

        self._update(2 * node + 1, m, r, ql, qr, v)
        self.tree[node] = self.tree[2 * node] + self.tree[2 * node + 1]

        if qr <= l or r <= ql:
            return 0
        if ql <= l and r <= qr:
            return self.tree[node]

        self._push(node, l, r)
        m = (l + r) // 2
        return self._query(2 * node, l, m, ql, qr) + self._query(2 * node + 1, m, r, ql, qr)

        if l >= r:

        self._update(1, 0, self.n, l, r, v)

        if l >= r:
            return 0
        return self._query(1, 0, self.n, l, r)

# Define a helper to collect stdout and stderr
def capture_output(f):
    @wraps(f)
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        old_stdout = sys.stdout
        old_stderr = sys.stderr
        sys.stdout = sys.stderr = open('devnull', 'w')
        try:
            result = f(*args, **kwargs)
        finally:
            sys.stdout = old_stdout
            sys.stderr = old_stderr
        return result
    return wrapper

class Heap:
    def __init__(self, compare):
        self.compare = compare
        self.data = []  # list of (item, insertion_counter) pairs
        self._seq = 0

    def _cmp(self, i, j):
        a, ai = self.data[i]
        b, bj = self.data[j]
        c = self.compare(a, b)
        if c != 0:
            return c
        return ai - bj

    def push(self, item):
        self._seq += 1
        self.data.append((item, self._seq))
        self._sift_up(len(self.data) - 1)

    def pop(self):
        if not self.data:
            raise IndexError('pop from empty heap')
        top = self.data[0][0]
        last = self.data.pop()
        if self.data:
            self.data[0] = last
            self._sift_down(0)
        return top

    def peek(self):
        return self.data[0][0]

    def _sift_up(self, i):
        while i > 0:
            parent = (i - 1) // 2
            if self._cmp(i, parent) < 0:
                self.data[i], self.data[parent] = self.data[parent], self.data[i]
                i = parent
            else:
                break

    def _sift_down(self, i):
        n = len(self.data)
        while True:
            left = 2 * i + 1
            right = 2 * i + 2
            smallest = i
            if left < n and self._cmp(left, smallest) < 0:
                smallest = left
            if right < n and self._cmp(right, smallest) < 0:
                smallest = right
            if smallest == i:
                break
            self.data[i], self.data[smallest] = self.data[smallest], self.data[i]
            i = smallest

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data)

def build_decode_tree(code_table):
    root = [None, None]
    for sym, bits in code_table.items():
        node = root
        for b in bits[:-1]:
            idx = int(b)
            if node[idx] is None:
                node[idx] = [None, None]
            node = node[idx]
        last = int(bits[-1])
        if node[last] is not None:
            raise ValueError(f'Prefix collision at code {bits!r}')
        node[last] = sym
    return root

    out = []

    consumed = 0
    for byte in data:

            bit = (byte >> shift) & 1
            node = node[bit]
            if not isinstance(node, list):

                node = root
            consumed += 1
    return out

def build_decode_tree(code_table):
    root = [None, None]
    for sym, bits in code_table.items():

            node = node[idx]
        last = int(bits[-1])
        if node[last] is not None:

    out = []
    node = root
    consumed = 0
    for byte in data:

            if consumed > bit_length:
                return out
            bit = (byte >> shift) & 1
            node = node[bit]
            if not isinstance(node, list):
                out.append(node)
                node = root
            consumed += 1
    return out

turns.response_text:
The bug is that `huffman_decode` continues reading bits past the `bit_length` even after `consumed` exceeds it. The `if consumed > bit_length` check is inside the `for shift` loop, which means it only stops processing the current byte when `consumed > bit_length` (i.e., after the bit is consumed). This causes the loop to continue reading bits beyond `bit_length`.

Here's the fix: move the check outside the `for shift` loop so that it stops reading the current byte when `consumed > bit_length`.

```python
def huffman_decode(code_table, data, bit_length):
    root = build_decode_tree(code_table)
    out = []
    node = root
    consumed = 0
    for byte in data:
        if consumed > bit_length:
            break
        for shift in range(7, -1, -1):
            bit = (byte >> shift) & 1
            node = node[bit]
            if not isinstance(node, list):
                out.append(node)
                node = root
            consumed += 1
    return out
```

def build_decode_tree(code_table):
    """Build a binary tree: each internal node is a 2-list [left, right];
    each leaf is the symbol (any object).
    """
    root = [None, None]
    for sym, bits in code_table.items():
        node = root
        for b in bits[:-1]:
            idx = int(b)
            if node[idx] is None:
                node[idx] = [None, None]
            node = node[idx]
        last = int(bits[-1])
        if node[last] is not None:
            raise ValueError(f'Prefix collision at code {bits!r}')
        node[last] = sym
    return root

    """Decode a bit-stream (stored as `data` bytes, with `bit_length` bits total,
    MSB-first within each byte) into a list of symbols using the canonical
    Huffman code table.
    """
    root = build_decode_tree(code_table)

turns.response_text:
The current implementation doesn't handle the case when the bit stream ends in the middle of a byte, leading to reading beyond the actual bit length. Let me fix that.

I'll modify the loop to stop processing bytes once the total bit length is reached, ensuring we don't read beyond the specified `bit_length`.

```python
def build_decode_tree(code_table):
    """Build a binary tree: each internal node is a 2-list [left, right];
    each leaf is the symbol (any object).
    """
    root = [None, None]
    for sym, bits in code_table.items():
        node = root
        for b in bits[:-1]:
            idx = int(b)
            if node[idx] is None:
                node[idx] = [None, None]
            node = node[idx]
        last = int(bits[-1])
        if node[last] is not None:
            raise ValueError(f'Prefix collision at code {bits!r}')
        node[last] = sym
    return root


def huffman_decode(code_table, data, bit_length):
    """Decode a bit-stream (stored as `data` bytes, with `bit_length` bits total,
    MSB-first within each byte) into a list of symbols using the canonical
    Huffman code table.
    """
    root = build_decode_tree(code_table)
    out = []
    node = root
    consumed = 0
    for byte in data:
        for shift in range(7, -1, -1):
            if consumed >= bit_length:
                break
            bit = (byte >> shift) & 1
            node = node[bit]
            if not isinstance(node, list):
                out.append(node)
                node = root
            consumed += 1
    return out
```

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "class RingBuffer:
    def __init__(self, capacity):
        if capacity <= 0:
            raise ValueError('capacity must be positive')
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.buf = [None] * capacity
        self.r = 0
        self.w = 0
        self.size = 0

    def write(self, x):
        self.buf[self.w] = x
        self.w = (self.w + 1) % self.capacity
        if self.size < self.capacity:
            self.size += 1
        else:
            self.r = (self.r + 1) % self.capacity

    def read(self):
        if self.size == 0:
            raise IndexError('read from empty buffer')
        x = self.buf[self.r]
        self.r = (self.r + 1) % self.capacity
        self.size -= 1
        return x

    def peek(self):
        if self.size == 0:
            raise IndexError('peek on empty buffer')
        return self.buf[self.r]

    def __len__(self):
        return self.size", "test_code": "# Test 1: Simple FIFO within capacity

rb.write(3)

assert rb.read() == 2
assert rb.read() == 3
assert len(rb) == 0

# Test 2: Empty read raises

try:
    rb.read()
    assert False, 'Expected IndexError'
except IndexError:
    pass

# Test 3: Overwrite drops oldest — the key fix

rb.write(1)
rb.write(2)
rb.write(3)
rb.write(4)  # overwrites 1
assert len(rb) == 3
assert rb.read() == 2
assert rb.read() == 3
assert rb.read() == 4
assert len(rb) == 0

# Test 4: Multiple overwrites in a row

for x in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]:

assert rb.read() == 4
assert rb.read() == 5

rb.write(1); rb.write(2)

rb.write(5); rb.write(6)  # now: 3,4,5,6 attempted; capacity 3 — overwrites 3

assert rb.read() == 6

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "class RingBuffer:
    """Fixed-capacity single-reader single-writer ring buffer.
    Writes never block; when full, the oldest element is dropped (overwrite).
    """

    def __init__(self, capacity):
        if capacity <= 0:
            raise ValueError('capacity must be positive')
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.buf = [None] * capacity
        self.r = 0
        self.w = 0
        self.size = 0

    def write(self, x):
        self.buf[self.w] = x
        self.w = (self.w + 1) % self.capacity
        if self.size < self.capacity:
            self.size += 1
        else:
            # Advance read pointer on overwrite
            self.r = (self.r + 1) % self.capacity

    def read(self):
        if self.size == 0:
            raise IndexError('read from empty buffer')
        x = self.buf[self.r]
        self.r = (self.r + 1) % self.capacity
        self.size -= 1
        return x

    def peek(self):
        if self.size == 0:
            raise IndexError('peek on empty buffer')
        return self.buf[self.r]

    def __len__(self):
        return self.size", "test_code": "print('All RingBuffer tests passed!')"}}

def truncate_graphemes(s, n):
    if n <= 0:

    clusters = []
    i = 0

```python
import unicodedata

def truncate_graphemes(s, n):
```
    """Truncate `s` to at most `n` grapheme clusters. Never split a cluster."""
    if n <= 0:
        return ''
    clusters = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(s) and len(clusters) < n:
        cluster = []
        # Base character starts a new cluster
        if not cluster or unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'Lo':
            cluster.append(s[i])
            i += 1
        # Combine combining marks
        while i < len(s):
            c = s[i]
            cat = unicodedata.category(c)
            if cat.startswith('M'):
                cluster.append(c)
                i += 1
            elif c == '\u200d':  # ZWJ joins next char into cluster
                cluster.append(c)
                i += 1
                if i < len(s):
                    cluster.append(s[i])
                    i += 1
            elif c == '\U0001F1E6' or c == '\U0001F1FF':  # Regional Indicator
                if i + 1 < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i+1]) == 'Lo':
                    cluster.append(c)
                    cluster.append(s[i+1])
                    i += 2
                else:
                    cluster.append(c)
                    i += 1
            elif c == '\U0001F1E6' and i + 1 < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i+1]) == 'Lo':
                # Regional Indicator pair
                cluster.append(c)
                cluster.append(s[i+1])
                i += 2
            elif c == '\U000FE00' or c == '\U000FE0F':  # Variation Selector
                cluster.append(c)
                i += 1
            else:
                break
        clusters.append(''.join(cluster))
    return ''.join(clusters[:n])

# Test 2: Combining marks extend the base
# é as 'e' + U+0301 should be one grapheme

assert truncate_graphemes(s, 1) == 'é'
assert truncate_graphemes(s, 2) == 'él'

import unicodedata

def truncate_graphemes(s, n):
    """Truncate `s` to at most `n` grapheme clusters. Never split a cluster."""
    if n <= 0:
        return ''
    clusters = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(s) and len(clusters) < n:
        cluster = []
        # Base character starts a new cluster
        if not cluster or unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'Lo':
            cluster.append(s[i])
            i += 1
        # Combine combining marks
        while i < len(s):
            c = s[i]
            cat = unicodedata.category(c)
            if cat.startswith('M'):
                cluster.append(c)
                i += 1
            elif c == '\u200d':  # ZWJ joins next char into cluster
                cluster.append(c)
                i += 1
                if i < len(s):
                    cluster.append(s[i])
                    i += 1
            elif c == '\U0001F1E6' or c == '\U0001F1FF':  # Regional Indicator
                if i + 1 < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i+1]) == 'Lo':
                    cluster.append(c)
                    cluster.append(s[i+1])
                    i += 2
                else:
                    cluster.append(c)
                    i += 1
            elif c == '\U0001F1E6' and i + 1 < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i+1]) == 'Lo':
                # Regional Indicator pair
                cluster.append(c)
                cluster.append(s[i+1])
                i += 2
            elif c == '\U000FE00' or c == '\U000FE0F':  # Variation Selector
                cluster.append(c)
                i += 1
            else:
                break
        clusters.append(''.join(cluster))
    return ''.join(clusters[:n])

```python
import unicodedata

def truncate_graphemes(s, n):
    """Truncate `s` to at most `n` grapheme clusters. Never split a cluster."""
    if n <= 0:
        return ''
    clusters = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(s) and len(clusters) < n:
        # Determine if current character starts a new cluster
        if unicodedata.category(s[i])[0] == 'M' or s[i] == '‍' or unicodedata.category(s[i])[0] == 'Z':
            # Combining mark, ZWJ, or Zero-Width Joiner — extend previous cluster
            clusters[-1] += s[i]
            i += 1
        elif unicodedata.category(s[i])[0] == 'Z' and i + 1 < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i + 1])[0] == 'Z':
            # Regional Indicator pair — treat as one cluster
            clusters.append(s[i] + s[i + 1])
            i += 2
        else:
            # Start new cluster
            clusters.append(s[i])
            i += 1
    return ''.join(clusters[:n])
```

turns.response_text:
The bug is in the logic for handling combining marks (`M` category) and variation selectors (`So`). The current code incorrectly appends combining marks to the current cluster and continues, but doesn't reset the cluster upon encountering a new base character. Also, variation selectors (`So`) are not properly handled in the context of grapheme clusters.

Here is the fixed implementation:

```python
import unicodedata

def truncate_graphemes(s, n):
    """Truncate `s` to at most `n` grapheme clusters. Never split a cluster."""
    if n <= 0:
        return ''
    clusters = []
    current_cluster = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(s) and len(clusters) < n:
        c = s[i]
        if unicodedata.category(c).startswith('M'):  # Combining mark
            current_cluster.append(c)
            i += 1
            continue
        elif c == '‍':  # Zero-Width Joiner
            if i + 1 < len(s):
                current_cluster.append(c)
                i += 1
                continue
        elif unicodedata.category(c).startswith('Zs') or unicodedata.category(c) == 'So':  # Regional Indicator or Variation Selector
            current_cluster.append(c)
            i += 1
            continue
        else:  # Base character or ZWJ (handled above)
            if current_cluster:
                clusters.append(''.join(current_cluster))
                current_cluster = []
            clusters.append(c)
            i += 1
    if current_cluster:
        clusters.append(''.join(current_cluster))
    return ''.join(clusters)
```

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def parse_cron_field(spec, lo, hi):
    """Parse a single cron field into a sorted, deduplicated list of ints."""
    out = set()
    for part in spec.split(','):
        if not part:
            raise ValueError(f'empty part in {spec!r}')
        step = 1
        if '/' in part:
            base, step_s = part.split('/', 1)
            step = int(step_s)
            if step < 1:
                raise ValueError(f'step must be >= 1 in {part!r}')
        else:
            base = part
        if base == '*':
            a, b = lo, hi
        elif '-' in base:
            a_s, b_s = base.split('-', 1)
            a, b = int(a_s), int(b_s)
        else:
            a = int(base)
            b = a if step == 1 else hi
        if a < lo or b > hi or a > b:
            raise ValueError(f'out-of-range in {part!r} (bounds {lo}..{hi})')
        # Bug: stop is `b` (exclusive), so values equal to b are dropped.
        # Fix 1: Use `b + 1` as stop to include b
        # Fix 2: Use `hi + 1` for `a/s` to reach hi inclusive
        for v in range(a, b + 1, step):
            out.add(v)
    return sorted(out)", "test_code": "def test_parse_cron_field():

    assert parse_cron_field('1-10/3', 0, 59) == [1, 4, 7, 10]

    assert parse_cron_field('0/10', 0, 59) == [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50]

    assert parse_cron_field('2-5', 0, 10) == [2, 3, 4, 5]
    assert parse_cron_field('3', 0, 10) == [3]
    assert parse_cron_field('0-4,2-6', 0, 10) == [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
    for bad in ['60', '5-70', '-1', '100', '']:

def test_parse_cron_field():
    assert parse_cron_field('*/5', 0, 59) == [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55]
    assert parse_cron_field('10-30/5', 0, 59) == [10, 15, 20, 25, 30]
    assert parse_cron_field('1-10/3', 0, 59) == [1, 4, 7, 10]
    assert parse_cron_field('0,15,30,45', 0, 59) == [0, 15, 30, 45]
    assert parse_cron_field('55/10', 0, 59) == [55]
    assert parse_cron_field('0/10', 0, 59) == [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
    assert parse_cron_field('1/2', 1, 7) == [1, 3, 5, 7]
    assert parse_cron_field('*', 0, 6) == [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
    assert parse_cron_field('2-5', 0, 10) == [2, 3, 4, 5]
    assert parse_cron_field('3', 0, 10) == [3]
    assert parse_cron_field('0-4,2-6', 0, 10) == [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
    for bad in ['60', '5-70', '-1', '100', '']:
        try:
            parse_cron_field(bad, 0, 59)
            assert False, f'Expected ValueError on {bad!r}'
        except ValueError:
            pass
    try:
        parse_cron_field('10-5', 0, 59)
        assert False, 'Expected ValueError on inverted range'
    except ValueError:
        pass
    for bad in ['*/0', '5-10/0', '*/-2']:
        try:
            parse_cron_field(bad, 0, 59)
            assert False, f'Expected ValueError on {bad!r}'
        except ValueError:

def parse_cron_field(spec, lo, hi):
    out = set()
    for part in spec.split(','): 
        if not part:
            raise ValueError(f'empty part in {spec!r}')
        step = 1
        if '/' in part:
            base, step_s = part.split('/', 1)
            step = int(step_s)
            if step < 1:
                raise ValueError(f'step must be >= 1 in {part!r}')
        else:
            base = part
        if base == '*':
            a, b = lo, hi
        elif '-' in base:
            a_s, b_s = base.split('-', 1)
            a, b = int(a_s), int(b_s)
        else:
            a = int(base)
            b = hi if step == 1 else hi + 1
        if a < lo or b > hi or a > b:
            raise ValueError(f'out-of-range in {part!r} (bounds {lo}..{hi})')
        # Fix stop to be b + 1 for inclusive upper
        for v in range(a, b + 1, step):
            out.add(v)
    return sorted(out)

# Test 1: Star/step
assert parse_cron_field('*/5', 0, 59) == [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55]

# Test 2: Range/step with inclusive upper
assert parse_cron_field('10-30/5', 0, 59) == [10, 15, 20, 25, 30]

# Test 3: Range/step where (b-a) % s != 0 (upper not reachable)

assert parse_cron_field('0,15,30,45', 0, 59) == [0, 15, 30, 45]

# Test 5: a/s — single start with step, must reach hi inclusive

assert parse_cron_field('0/10', 0, 59) == [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
assert parse_cron_field('1/2', 1, 7) == [1, 3, 5, 7]

# Test 6: Plain star

# Test 7: Plain range
assert parse_cron_field('2-5', 0, 10) == [2, 3, 4, 5]

# Test 8: Single value
assert parse_cron_field('3', 0, 10) == [3]

# Test 9: Deduplication across commas
assert parse_cron_field('0-4,2-6', 0, 10) == [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

# Test 10: Out-of-range

    out = set()

            b = hi if step == 1 else hi + 1
        if a < lo or b > hi or a > b:
            raise ValueError(f'out-of-range in {part!r} (bounds {lo}..{hi})')

        for v in range(a, b + 1, step):
            out.add(v)

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def _parse_accept(header):
    entries = []
    for raw in header.split(','): 
        raw = raw.strip()
        if not raw:
            continue
        parts = [p.strip() for p in raw.split(';')]
        mtype = parts[0]
        if '/' not in mtype:
            continue
        t, st = mtype.split('/', 1)
        q = 1.0
        for p in parts[1:]:
            if p.startswith('q='):
                try:
                    q = float(p[2:])
                except ValueError:
                    q = 1.0
        entries.append((t, st, q))
    return entries


def _specificity(t, st):
    if t == '*' and st == '*': 
        return 0
    if st == '*': 
        return 1
    return 2


def select_media_type(accept_header, available):
    entries = _parse_accept(accept_header)
    best = None
    best_q = -1.0
    for offered in available:
        ot, ost = offered.split('/', 1)
        # Collect all matching entries, pick the one with the highest specificity.
        matches = []
        for t, st, q in entries:
            if (t == ot or t == '*') and (st == ost or st == '*'):
                matches.append((q, _specificity(t, st)))
        if not matches:
            continue
        # Sort by q descending, then by specificity descending.
        matches.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], x[1]), reverse=True)
        q, spec = matches[0]
        if q > best_q:
            best_q = q
            best = offered
    if best_q <= 0:
        return None
    return best", "test_code": "def test_select_media_type():
    assert select_media_type('application/json', ['application/json', 'text/html']) == 'application/json'
    res = select_media_type('text/html, application/json; q=0.9', ['application/json', 'text/html'])
    assert res == 'text/html', f'Got {res!r}'
    res = select_media_type('text/html; q=0.5, */*', ['text/html', 'application/json'])
    assert res == 'application/json', f'Got {res!r}'
    res = select_media_type('text/*; q=0.8, text/html; q=0.8', ['text/plain', 'text/html'])
    assert res == 'text/html', f'Got {res!r}'
    res = select_media_type('text/html; q=0, application/json; q=0.5', ['text/html'])
    assert res is None, f'Got {res!r}'
    res = select_media_type('text/html; q=0, application/json', ['text/html', 'application/json'])
    assert res == 'application/json', f'Got {res!r}'
    res = select_media_type('image/png', ['text/html', 'application/json'])
    assert res is None
    res = select_media_type('text/html, application/json', ['application/json', 'text/html'])
    assert res == 'application/json', f'Got {res!r}'
    res = select_media_type('*/*', ['application/json', 'text/html'])
    assert res == 'application/json'
    res = select_media_type('text/html; level=1; q=0.8, text/html; q=0.4', ['text/html'])
    assert res == 'text/html'
    assert select_media_type('*/*', []) is None
    res = select_media_type('text/*; q=0.5, application/json; q=0.9, */*; q=0.1', ['text/html', 'application/json', 'application/xml'])
    assert res == 'application/json', f'Got {res!r}'

    print('All select_media_type tests passed!')

test_select_media_type()"}}
